Document [0] (Title: Portacaval Shunt -- Indications) An emergent esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) is crucial in the diagnosis and therapy for a suspected acute variceal bleed. Its usefulness is in the identification of the presence of varices as well as an exclusion for other sources of bleeding, for example, gastritis, ulcers, portal hypertensive gastropathy. Endoscopic interventions are effective in controlling hemorrhage and almost 90% of patients including sclerotherapy or variceal band ligation. If this fails, luminal tamponade with Sengstaken-Blakemore or Minnesota tubes can be utilized, both of which have large round gastric balloons that can be used to compress the GE junction and decrease blood flow to esophageal varices when placed on traction with a distal port to allow for the evacuation of luminal contents within the stomach. You wary of aspiration pneumonia as it is the most common complication of luminal tamponade, but elective endotracheal intubation is recommended as it can prevent aspiration especially in patients who have encephalopathy.
Document [1] (Title: Sengstaken-Blakemore Tube -- Continuing Education Activity) Acute esophageal variceal bleeding constitutes a life-threatening complication of portal hypertension, which most commonly arises from cirrhosis. Portal hypertension increases pressure within the portal venous system, leading to the formation of dilated submucosal veins in the distal esophagus, which are susceptible to rupture. Risk factors include chronic liver disease, alcohol use, viral hepatitis, and coagulopathy. Patients typically present with hematemesis, melena, hypotension, tachycardia, and signs of hypovolemic shock. Initial management involves hemodynamic stabilization, vasoactive medications, prophylactic antibiotics, and urgent endoscopic therapy such as band ligation or sclerotherapy.
Document [2] (Title: Gastric Varices -- Treatment / Management) Surgical Approach With the advent of newer minimally invasive and improved diagnostic and therapeutic techniques via endoscopy and interventional radiology, the role of surgery in managing acute gastric variceal bleeding is limited and should be considered a last resort when the above measures fail. However, splenectomy or splenic artery embolization should be considered a means of definitive therapy in patients presenting with acute gastric variceal bleeding secondary to sinistral or left-sided portal hypertension caused by splenic vein thrombosis (SVT). Splenectomy eliminates collateral venous flow and prevents future gastric variceal bleeding secondary to splenic vein thrombosis. [22]

Document [4] (Title: Sengstaken-Blakemore Tube -- Anatomy and Physiology) Esophageal varices are a direct manifestation of chronic portal hypertension, which most commonly results from cirrhosis. Progressive fibrosis, architectural distortion, and regenerative nodules obstruct normal portal venous outflow in the cirrhotic liver. This resistance elevates pressure within the portal system, diverting blood through collateral pathways connecting the portal and systemic circulations. [7] One of the most clinically significant networks involves the left gastric vein (coronary vein), which drains into the portal vein and communicates with the esophageal venous plexus. These submucosal and paraesophageal veins further connect with the systemic azygos and hemiazygos veins, forming fragile, thin-walled varices in the distal esophagus and proximal stomach. [8]
Document [5] (Title: Esophageal Varices -- Introduction) Esophageal varices are dilated submucosal distal esophageal veins connecting the portal and systemic circulations. This happens due to portal hypertension (most commonly a result of cirrhosis), resistance to portal blood flow, and increased portal venous blood inflow. The most common fatal complication of cirrhosis is variceal rupture; the severity of liver disease correlates with the presence of varices and risk of bleeding. [1] [2] [3] [4]

Document [12] (Title: Esophageal Varices -- Epidemiology) Incidence At diagnosis, 30% of cirrhotic patients have varices which increase to 90% in 10 years. The 1-year rate of first variceal bleeding is 5% for small varices, 15% for large varices. Portal hypertension is common in chronic liver disease (CLD) in children. Prevalence It is more common in males than in females. Fifty percent of patients with esophageal varices will experience bleeding at some point. Variceal bleeding has a 10% to 20% mortality rate in the 6 weeks following the episode. In the West, the two common causes of portal hypertension are alcohol and viral hepatitis. In Asia and Africa, the most common causes of portal hypertension include schistosomiasis and hepatitis B/C.
Document [13] (Title: Sengstaken-Blakemore Tube -- Contraindications) Sengstaken-Blakemore tube insertion has no absolute contraindications when used to manage massive variceal hemorrhage urgently. In cases of profound hemodynamic instability or uncontrolled bleeding, the urgency of achieving hemostasis outweighs most procedural concerns. However, several relative contraindications should be considered, particularly when alternative methods of hemorrhage control are available, and time permits interprofessional consultation. These conditions include the following: Known esophageal strictures or stenosis Recent upper gastrointestinal or esophageal surgery Large or complicated hiatal hernias Unresolved severe coagulopathy Active esophageal ulceration or mucosal disruption Recent endoscopic variceal interventions (eg, band ligation or sclerotherapy within the preceding 7-10 days) [11] [12]
Document [14] (Title: Gastric Varices -- Introduction) Gastric varices are dilated submucosal collateral veins that develop in the setting of portal hypertension due to any etiology with or without cirrhosis. [1] Compared to esophageal varices, gastric varices are less common occurring in approximately 20% of cirrhotic patients. However, gastric varices have a higher propensity to bleed severely and are often associated with poor patient outcomes. [2] [3] Based on their stomach location, gastric varices are classified as gastroesophageal varices (GOV) and isolated gastric varices (IGV).

Document [17] (Title: Portal Hypertension -- Treatment / Management -- Risk Management) In cases of variceal bleeding, immediate resuscitation with intravenous fluids and placement of 2 large-bore intravenous (IV) lines is essential, followed by admission to an intensive care unit. Transfusion should be initiated when hemoglobin levels fall below 7 g/dL. Medications that reduce splanchnic blood flow—vasopressin, terlipressin, somatostatin, or octreotide—should be considered. [26] [27] Octreotide is typically given as a 50 µg IV bolus followed by a continuous infusion of 50 µg/hour for 3 to 5 days. Somatostatin is administered as a 250 µg bolus followed by an infusion at 250 µg/hour over the same period. Terlipressin is administered as a 2 mg IV bolus, followed by 1 to 2 mg every 4 to 6 hours until hemostasis is achieved, or for up to 5 days. [28]
Document [18] (Title: Sengstaken-Blakemore Tube -- Indications) The Sengstaken-Blakemore tube is indicated as a rescue intervention for acute, uncontrolled upper gastrointestinal bleeding secondary to esophageal varices, particularly when standard therapies are unavailable, delayed, or unsuccessful. The primary purpose of this device is to achieve temporary hemostasis in massive variceal hemorrhage that does not respond to pharmacologic agents, such as octreotide, vasopressin, and somatostatin analogs, or endoscopic interventions, including variceal band ligation or sclerotherapy. In modern practice, the tube is most often used in emergent settings when endoscopy is not immediately available, such as when treatment occurs in rural hospitals, patients are in transit to tertiary centers, or persistent hemodynamic instability precludes immediate endoscopy.
Document [19] (Title: Portacaval Shunt -- Technique or Treatment) A side-to-side anastomosis and a portacaval shunt have been preferred by some surgeons in the presence of portal hypertension with no evidence of an increase in pressure on the hepatic and of the temporary occluded portal vein. This is suggestive that the arterial supply was going to the liver and that decreasing the portal pressure via this anastomosis with the inferior vena cava will not result in the diversion of arterial supply provided to the liver. An advantage to type of shunt is the fact that it provides decompression to the hepatic sinusoids, which have the potential to be beneficial in treating patients who have intractable ascites coinciding with variceal hemorrhage. Using the portacaval shunt in treatment for refractory ascites is not a universally accepted indication for the procedure, although several studies suggest that it may, in fact, be an effective model for therapy. A side-to-side portacaval shunt is preferred when attempting to control refractory ascites.

[29] Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily is recommended for 7 days, with a potential switch to oral ciprofloxacin 400 mg twice daily or levofloxacin 500 mg daily. Endoscopy should be performed within 12 hours of admission. Endoscopic variceal ligation is indicated for large varices or varices with high-risk stigmata. However, diverting portal blood flow reduces hepatic perfusion, increasing the risk of hepatic encephalopathy.

Document [27] (Title: Portacaval Shunt -- Indications) A clinically significant manifestation of portal hypertension is variceal hemorrhage, which has substantial morbidity and mortality. Portal hypertension causes vascular channel dilatation in collateral portosystemic channels which arise where the systemic veins and portal veins meet. A common site of varices includes gastroesophageal with portal inflow coming from the left gastric vein and short gastric veins and systemic outflow from the intercostal, diaphragmatic, and esophageal veins. Collateral sites also include hemorrhoids via the superior hemorrhoidal vein and middle and inferior hemorrhoidal veins, caput medusa a where the left portal vein and umbilical and abdominal wall veins meet. In patients with cirrhosis, 50% have the presence of gastroesophageal varices. An annual rate of variceal hemorrhage is estimated to be between 5% to 15%, with size being the dominant predicting factor of hemorrhage.

It is identified as a member of the group of conditions that precede or contribute to pre‑eclampsia (“مقدمات تسمم الحمل”), underscoring its relevance as a risk factor in pregnancy‑related hypertensive disorders. The same hypertension SMQ is also listed as a member of the set of indications for renal revascularization surgery (“جراحة إعادة التوعي الكلوي”), reflecting its role in the evaluation and management of renal vascular disease. Essential hypertension, a primary form of high blood pressure, explicitly includes the hypertension SMQ as one of its components, reinforcing the classification of the SMQ within the broader spectrum of essential hypertension. In addition, benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis—a form of chronic, non‑inflammatory arteriolar damage in the kidneys—is described as clinically similar to essential hypertension, indicating overlapping pathophysiological features and potential shared therapeutic considerations. Together, these relationships highlight how hypertension serves as a common thread linking pregnancy complications, renal vascular interventions, and renal arteriolar pathology, emphasizing its importance in comprehensive patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1786) Values that are “Above reference range” are considered “Increased.” An “Increased” measurement is the typical interpretation of “essential hypertension,” reflecting elevated blood pressure. “Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis” is clinically similar to “essential hypertension” and is categorized under the parent condition “اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية” (renal vascular disorder). Both “اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية” and “تسلخ الشرايين” (arterial ulceration) have the “Vascular System” listed as their associated anatomic site, highlighting the central role of the vascular system in these renal and arterial pathologies. Recognizing the connections among elevated values, essential hypertension, benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis, and their shared vascular involvement aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of patients with high‑blood‑pressure–related vascular disorders.
Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2858) Primary hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is a chronic condition that commonly presents with elevated mean arterial pressure. It is closely linked to hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), which is considered a severe manifestation of the same underlying disorder. Primary hypertension is also associated with primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), indicating a possible overlap in vascular pathology. Additionally, primary hypertension has a recognized relationship with metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض), reflecting shared risk factors and pathophysiological mechanisms. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians assess cardiovascular risk, monitor for acute hypertensive emergencies, evaluate pulmonary vascular involvement, and address metabolic abnormalities in patients with primary hypertension.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2309) Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is characterized by the manifestations of elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated systolic blood pressure, and elevated mean arterial pressure. This condition is conceptually linked to primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ) and is clinically associated with gout (مرض النقرس). Essential hypertension is also mapped to a hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم) as well as to diastolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي), reflecting its role as a foundational vascular disorder. Within the broader vascular system, the elevated diastolic, mean arterial, and systolic blood pressure states are considered child entities, and the vascular hierarchy further includes hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, diastolic hypertension, and systolic hypertension as subordinate concepts. This integrated framework underscores how essential hypertension underlies multiple pressure‑related vascular conditions and its relevance to comorbidities such as gout, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in patient care.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 684) Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي, ضغط الدم) is a primary form of high blood pressure characterized by elevated systolic, diastolic, and mean arterial pressures. It is clinically similar to benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis and shares renal vascular changes, while the more severe malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is linked to the broader category “hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension.” Essential hypertension includes the subtypes diastolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي), the latter being a vascular disorder within the vascular domain. The condition may progress to hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم) and is associated with primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ). Abnormal chest radiographs (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) can be observed in patients with essential hypertension and are also associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ), indicating that hepatic disease may produce atypical pulmonary imaging findings. The vascular system therefore encompasses elevated pressure measurements, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, diastolic and systolic hypertension, and their renal and hepatic interrelationships, informing comprehensive assessment and management of patients with hypertension and related vascular, renal, and hepatic conditions.

Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808) Primary hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is defined by the presence of elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure, each of which is a direct manifestation of this condition. Within the vascular domain, these elevated pressure measurements are considered vascular children, reflecting the systemic impact of primary hypertension on the circulatory system. Primary hypertension is also mapped to several related vascular entities: hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), diastolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي), and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي). Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1818) Primary hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is characterized by several hemodynamic manifestations, including elevated systolic blood pressure, elevated diastolic blood pressure, and elevated mean arterial pressure. These elevated pressure measurements are considered vascular findings and are organized under the broader vascular system, which also encompasses related disorders such as hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), reflex hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي), and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي). The vascular hierarchy places the elevated systolic, diastolic, and mean arterial pressures as child entities of the vascular category, while the vascular category itself includes the aforementioned hypertension‑related conditions as its child entities. This integrated view highlights how primary hypertension manifests through specific pressure elevations and is closely linked to other vascular hypertension subtypes and acute hypertensive events, underscoring the importance of comprehensive vascular assessment in patient care.
